elements.sent-2411-007: Cherry Avenue, Tucson, 85721, USA \and Space Telescope Science Institute, 3700 San Martin Dr., Baltimore, MD 21218, USA \and Department of Physics \& Astronomy, Johns Hopkins University, 3400 N Charles St, Baltimore, MD 21218 \abstract {Understanding the dust content of galaxies, its evolution with redshift and its relationship to stars and star formation is fundamental for our understanding of galaxy evolution.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Dust acts as both a catalyst of star formation and as a shield for star light.
elements.sent-2411-009: State-of-the-art millimeter ground-based facilities like ALMA have made dust observation ever more accessible, even at high redshift.
elements.sent-2411-010: However dust emission is typically very faint, making the use of stacking techniques instrumental in the study of dust in statistically sound samples.
elements.sent-2411-011: The large field of view provided by the ALCS allows us to reach a final sample of $\sim4000$ galaxies with known coordinates and SED-derived physical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-012: We stack sources with SFR between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{3}$ M$_\odot$ per year, and stellar mass between $10^{8}$ and $10^{12}$ M$_\odot$, splitting them in different stellar mass and SFR bins.
elements.sent-2411-013: Through stacking we retrieve the continuum 1.2\,mm flux, a known dust mass tracer, allowing us to derive the dust mass evolution with redshift and its relation with SFR and stellar mass.
elements.sent-2411-014: From the non detections we derive 3-$\sigma$ upper limits.
elements.sent-2411-015: We observe a steady decline in the average dust mass with redshift.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, sources with higher stellar mass or SFR have higher dust mass on average, allowing us to derive scaling relations.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our results are mostly in good agreement with models at $z\sim1$ -- $3$, but indicate typically lower dust-mass than predicted at higher redshift.
elements.sent-2411-018: Dust impacts both the direct evolution of galaxies as well as their observations in multiple ways.
elements.sent-2411-019: It is thought to be the main catalyst of H$_2$ formation \citep{Wakelam2017 , one of the main components of the molecular clouds in the interstellar medium (ISM) and hence one of the principal drivers of star formation \citep[e.g.][]{Scoville2013 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In addition to the direct effect on the physical properties of galaxies, dust also plays a critical role in astrophysical observations.
elements.sent-2411-021: The dust grains attenuating the starlight are consequently heated, and in turn re-radiate the energy at longer wavelengths, this is the so-called dust-obscured star formation \citep[see for e.g.][]{Casey2014,Hodge2020,Zavala2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: As a consequence, the light coming from star-forming regions is typically dominated by dust continuum emission.
elements.sent-2411-023: The relationship between dust mass and other physical characteristics of galaxies have been intensely studied, for example, by studying dust-to-gas (atomic and molecular) or dust-to-metal ratios \citep[e.g.][]{Hunt2005,Draine2007,Engelbracht2008,Galametz2011,Magrini2011,Saintonge2013,Remy-Ruyer2014,Combes2018,Li2019,Shapley2020,Tacconi2020,Birkin2021,Popping2022,Popping2023 , or dust mass versus star formation rate (SFR) \citep[e.g.][]{DaCunha2010,Casey2012,Santini2014,Dudzeviciute2020,Dudzeviciute2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Recent studies have shown that the proportion of dust-obscured star formation has evolved with redshift \citep{Bouwens2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: It has been estimated to be a dominant fraction during the peak of the cosmic star formation history.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, it appears to have accounted for only $\sim$ 20\% -- 25\% of the total star formation at redshift $z=6$ -- $7$ \citep[see][]{Zavala2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Similarly, the overall cosmic dust density has been evidenced to peak around $z\sim 1-2$ with a rapid decline at higher redshift \citep[see for example][]{Driver2018,Magnelli2020,Pozzi2020,Dudzeviciute2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Furthermore, massive dust reservoirs have been detected in massive high-redshift galaxies \citep[e.g.][]{Bertoldi2003,Valiante2009,Venemans2012,Watson2015,Laporte2017,Tamura2019,Tamura2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: While this is probably not the case for more typical, less massive high-redshift galaxies, it highlights the importance of studying the evolution of dust mass with redshift.
elements.sent-2411-030: Millimeter or sub-millimeter emission has been proposed to be a tracer of dust mass \citep{Scoville2014,Scoville2016,Scoville2017 , with the assumption that the emission is optically thin and measured far from the peak of the dust spectral energy distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The Atacama Large Millimeter/Submillimeter Array (ALMA) has become instrumental in the quest to study dust mass in lower-mass galaxies and its evolution in the mm and sub-mm bands.
elements.sent-2411-032: With large surveys and high-redshift single target observations becoming progressively more accessible \citep[e.g.][]{Knudsen2016,Walter2016,Gonzalez-Lopez2017,Gonzalez-Lopez2020,Laporte2017,Bethermin2020,Aravena2020 the evolution of dust is studied in increasingly statistically sound samples.}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, observations of high-redshift galaxies are typically biased by construction towards the observation of the brightest sources (Malmquist bias).
elements.sent-2411-034: To draw a complete picture of dust evolution with redshift it is necessary to also study galaxies with lower intrinsic luminosities.
elements.sent-2411-035: Gravitational lensing can be used as a tool to enhance the signal from faint galaxies without the need for excessive integration time.
elements.sent-2411-036: Similarly, tools like stacking can statistically improve the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) drastically when studying large samples.
elements.sent-2411-037: With the help of both gravitational lensing and stacking, it is hence possible to push the limit of observations toward sources with lower intrinsic dust luminosity.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this paper we present the stacking analysis of 10386 gravitationally lensed galaxies from the ALMA Lensing Cluster Survey (ALCS) at $z>1$ (cluster and field sources at $z\lesssim1$ are studied in a separate paper: \citet{Guerrero .}
elements.sent-2411-039: By binning galaxies by redshift, and further splitting them according to their stellar mass or SFR, we study the evolution of the dust mass with redshift, and its scaling relation with stellar mass and SFR.
elements.sent-2411-040: Besides, we also integrate the contribution from all galaxies --in each redshift bin-- to assess the evolution of the cosmic dust density.
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section \ref{sec:data we describe the overall data set, the catalog and the different subsamples.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Section \ref{sec:method we describe both the stacking method, performed using LineStacker \citep{Jolly2020 , as well as the processes involved in the dust mass calculation.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section \ref{sec:results we present our results and discuss them in Section \ref{sec:discussion .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally in Section \ref{sec:summary we present a summary of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In the appendix we show alternative stacking procedures as well as some extra details on the main analysis.
elements.sent-2411-046: Throughout the paper, we assume a $\Lambda$CDM cosmology with $\Omega_m = 0.3$, $\Omega_\lambda = 0.7$ and $H_0=70$ \kms Mpc$^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: All magnitudes are quoted in the AB system, such that M$_{\mathrm{AB }$ = 23.9-2.5 log$_{10}$ (S$_\nu$ [$\mu$Jy]).}
elements.sent-2411-048: The ALMA Lensing Cluster Survey (ALCS) is a large ALMA program accepted in cycle 6 (Project ID: 2018.1.00035.L; PI: K.. Kohno).
elements.sent-2411-049: It observed 33 lensing clusters in band 6 ($\lambda\sim1.2\,$mm), spanning $\sim$\,110\,arcmin$^2$ (primary beam (PB) $>0.5$).
elements.sent-2411-050: The clusters are distributed as follow: 16 from RELICS \citep[the Reionization Lensing Cluster Survey][]{Coe2019 , 12 from CLASH \citep[the Cluster Lensing And Supernova survey with Hubble][]{Postman2012 , and 5 from the Frontier Fields survey \citep{Lotz2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Observations were carried out between December 2018 and December 2019 (cycles 6 and 7) in compact array configurations C43-1 and C43-2, in a double frequency window setup, observing both from 250.0 to 257.5\,GHz and from 265.0 to 272.5\,GHz, for a total bandwidth of 15\,GHz.
elements.sent-2411-052: When available, the ALCS data were concatenated with existing ALMA data, notably the ALMA Frontier Fields Survey (Project ID: 2013.1.00999.S, PI: Bauer and Project ID; 2015.1.01425.S: PI: Bauer).
elements.sent-2411-053: The data were reduced and calibrated using the Common Astronomy Software Applications \citep[CASA,][]{CASA package version 5.4.0 for the 26 clusters observed in cycle 6 and v5.6.1 for the remaining clusters observed in cycle 7.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Throughout this paper, we use natural-weighted, primary-beam-corrected, and {\it uv -tapered continuum maps, with a tapering parameter of 2\,arcsec (the full width at half maximum of the synthesized beam is 2\,arcsec; with a corresponding pixel size of 0.16 arcsec).}
elements.sent-2411-055: The average RMS of the maps is $\sim63\, \mathrm{\mu}$Jy/beam, see Table \ref{tab:cluster_sources for the detailed RMS of each map.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Full description of the survey can be found in \citet{KohnoALCS .}

elements.sent-2411-081: Stacking was done using LineStacker \citep{Jolly2020 , in single channel mode (to perform continuum stacking), on {\it uv -tapered images (see Section \ref{sec:ALCS ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Sources were stacked pixel to pixel using mean stacks without weights (median stacks are also performed, see Section \ref{sec:median_stacking ).}
elements.sent-2411-083: Stacking was operated with stamp sizes of 9.76 $\times$ 9.76 arcsec$^2$ (61 $\times$ 61 pixels).
elements.sent-2411-084: Sources were spatially aligned using the position extracted from the \citet{Kokorev2022 catalog.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Stacked flux in Jy was retrieved by integrating the continuum flux in a central circular region of the stack stamp, with a radius $R_{\rm integ =2''$ (12.5 pixels), corresponding to the synthesized beam size, and dividing it by the beam size in pixel units.}
elements.sent-2411-086: To compute the RMS associated to each stacked cube a random set of source-free coordinates are drawn for each stacking position.
elements.sent-2411-087: An empty stack is then generated from the set of random coordinates \citep[see][]{Jolly2020 with the same characteristics as the normal stacks (i.e. stamp size and number of targets).}
elements.sent-2411-088: This process is performed 1000 times for each subsample.
elements.sent-2411-089: The standard deviation across all stack stamps is then computed and used to derive the RMS associated to each stack cube.
elements.sent-2411-090: When the flux in the central region of the stack stamp is lower than 3 times its associated RMS, a $3 \sigma$ upper limit is used in place of the integrated flux to compute $M_{\mathrm{dust }$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The averaged 1.2\,mm fluxes extracted from each stack maps are corrected for the mean magnification of sources in the stack subsample, the final flux is hence computed as: $F_{final = \frac{F_{stack {\overline{\mu }$, where $F_{final}$ is the final flux used to compute the dust mass (see Section \ref{sec:dust ), $F_{stack}$ is the flux obtained from the stacked map, and $\overline{\mu}$ is the average magnification of each source in the subsample.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Alternatively, each source could be corrected for its magnification before stacking: $F_{final = F_{\mathrm{corrected\ stack }$, where $F_{\mathrm{corrected\ stack }$ is the flux obtained from the stack of the individually magnification-corrected maps: $\mathrm{Map _{\mathrm{stack = \sum_{i=1 ^n \frac{\mathrm{Stamp _{i {\mu_{i }$, where $\mathrm{Map _{\mathrm{stack }$ is the stacked map obtained from individually magnification-corrected stamps (from which $F_{\mathrm{corrected\ stack }$ is obtained), $n$ is the number of sources in the subsample, $\mathrm{Stamp _{i}$ is the map-stamp associated to source $i$ and $\mu_i$ is the magnification associated to source $i$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The reason why we decided to correct for the average magnification of the sample after stacking is the following: the signal in each stack stamp consists of both the flux from the source, which is gravitationally magnified, and some noise, which is independent of the magnification.
elements.sent-2411-094: By correcting each stack stamp for its associated magnification, one corrects both the flux from the source and the foreground noise.
elements.sent-2411-095: The efficiency of stacking is based on the fact that the noise is random and approximately similar in every stack stamp.
elements.sent-2411-096: By down-scaling the noise in each stamp by a different number (unrelated to the noise properties), one might effectively reduce the efficiency and reliability of the stacking analysis.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, by correcting the final stack with the average magnification, the analysis would be biased toward high-magnification sources.
elements.sent-2411-098: Correcting each stamp individually pre-stacking should avoid this bias, we hence repeated our analysis with this correction method.
elements.sent-2411-099: The 1.2\,mm fluxes derived in both cases are very consistent, with values overlapping within the error margins.
elements.sent-2411-100: Following \citet{Magnelli2020 and \citet{Pozzi2021 we decided to use a single cold component to account for the dust temperature, and used a mass weighted dust temperature of $T_{\rm rest =25$\,K.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: While the total dust in galaxies is thought to consist of a warmer ($20<T<60$\,K) and a colder ($T<30$\,K) component, studies of local galaxies have shown that the cold component is responsible for most of the dust budget \citep[from 96\% to 99\%, see][]{Orellana2017 and for the majority of the Rayleigh-Jeans emission \citep[see also][for a study of the contribution of the cold and warm dust components to the dust continuum emission at $z\sim2$]{Shivaei2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-102: We decided to adopt $T_{\rm rest =25$\,K at all redshifts probed by our analyses, but one should note that dust masses go as $T^{-1}$, meaning that our dust mass results are effectively highly dependent on the estimated dust temperature, and that the masses derived in our analyses could be off by factors of a few if the assumed dust temperature is not correct (see Section \ref{sec:discussion ).}}

elements.sent-2411-203: To study the evolution of the dust mass through cosmic time, we binned the sources in five different redshifts bins, and further also grouped sources according to their SFR and stellar mass, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-204: We performed continuum stacking on each of the subsamples using LineStacker \citep{Jolly2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Using the mean continuum stacked flux, we computed average dust masses.
elements.sent-2411-206: From the non detections we derive $3\sigma$ upper limits.
elements.sent-2411-207: Using both detections and upper limits we study the evolution trend of dust mass with redshift.
elements.sent-2411-208: We see clear indications for average dust mass diminution with redshift.
elements.sent-2411-209: Similarly we study the evolution of dust mass with stellar mass and SFR, in both cases we see a positive correlation.
elements.sent-2411-210: From our detections we derived relationships between dust mass and SFR or stellar mass in different redshift range.
elements.sent-2411-211: The results from our study are mostly consistent with results from modeling (at least at $z\sim1$) and from other similar studies.
elements.sent-2411-212: Our analysis allows us to probe regimes of stellar mass and SFR unexplored so far.
elements.sent-2411-213: The highest redshift bin shows mostly non-detections, and the corresponding upper limits indicate low average dust masses --even though the overall RMS is low-- when compared to other studies.
elements.sent-2411-214: This could be due to the different rest-wavelength probed at high-$z$, lying closer to the peak of dust emission.
elements.sent-2411-215: Or to the tendency of individual high-$z$ dust measurements to be biased towards very dust-bright objects.
elements.sent-2411-216: The linear trends observed between stellar mass and dust mass confirm that as galaxies evolve and form more stars, they also accumulate more dust.
elements.sent-2411-217: These dust grains play a vital role in catalyzing the formation of H$_2$, thus influencing the overall gas reservoir available for future star formation.
elements.sent-2411-218: Dust becomes an integral component of a self-perpetuating cycle, accumulating throughout the stars' life cycle and subsequently bolstering the SFR.
elements.sent-2411-219: In addition the inverse trend observed between average dust-mass and redshift, implies that the build-up of dust in galaxies over cosmic-time is a gradual process, aligning with the overall evolution of the SFR density \citep[e.g][]{Magnelli2020 .}

elements.sent-2411-232: To make sure that quiescent galaxies do not bias our analysis and the derived average dust masses, we repeat our stacking analysis after removing all sources with $\Delta$MS $<-0.5$.
elements.sent-2411-233: In total, 2157 galaxies are removed from the sample, leading to a final size of 1946 after removing quiescent galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-234: The results are shown on Figures \ref{fig:mdust_mass_dMS and \ref{fig:mdust_sfr_dMS .}}

Error bars are computed in the same way as on Figure \ref{fig:mdust_mstar}. } \label{fig:mdust_mass_dMS}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{mdust_mass_z_sfr_dMS.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.45\linewidth]{z_evo_sfr_dMS.pdf} \caption{Similar to Figure \ref{fig:mdust_mass_dMS} but for SFR instead of stellar mass. } \label{fig:mdust_sfr_dMS}
elements.sent-2411-235: The main difference appear on the stellar mass subsamples, where the derived dust masses are overall higher --especially in the high-stellar mass regime-- after excluding quiescent galaxies (although some bins appear now undetected, likely due to the lower number of sources stacked).
elements.sent-2411-236: This suggest that quiescent galaxies may be overall more dust-poor at given stellar mass, driving the average down when included in the entire sample.
elements.sent-2411-237: In any event, the overall trends observed in the main analysis seem to be confirmed even when removing sources with $\Delta$MS$<-0.5$.
elements.sent-2411-238: One can note that our dust mass measurements are also in better agreement with previous individual results when quiescent galaxies are excluded from the stacks.

elements.section-2411-011: Dust mass inferred from median stacking
elements.sent-2411-249: In addition to the mean stacking analyses we performed median stacking for each of the subsamples, in the same way as described in the main analysis.
elements.sent-2411-250: The median stacking stamps are shown on Figure \ref{fig:stamps_median and here we show the dust mass evolution as a function of stellar mass and redshift (Figure \ref{fig:mdust_mass_median ) and as a function of SFR and redshift (Figure \ref{fig:mdust_sfr_median ), similar to Figures \ref{fig:mdust_mstar and \ref{fig:mdust_sfr .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: While the median stacking analyses show typically lower dust masses (indicating possible skewed distributions) the results and trends are overall very consistent with the mean stacking analyses.
